nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or stagnant freshwater — swamps, rice paddies, irrigation channels, seasonal pools, and small streams with dense aquatic and marginal vegetation; often in low-oxygen conditions where air-breathing is essential

decorPreferences: dense floating plants (water lettuce, frogbit, Amazon frogbit) to reduce surface light and provide refuge
emergent or marginal plants along sides
driftwood and leaf litter for cover and tannin release
smooth substrate (fine sand or small gravel)
tight-fitting lid — mandatory; strong jumper and air-breather requiring surface access

diet.notes: Piscivore-leaning carnivore. Wild diet consists primarily of small fish, invertebrates, worms, and aquatic insects. In captivity, feed meaty foods: earthworms, bloodworms, prawns/shrimp, mussels, small whole fish (lancefish, whitebait), and high-protein frozen foods. Most specimens can be weaned onto carnivore pellets (e.g. Hikari Massivore, New Life Spectrum) with patience. Live feeder fish should be avoided due to disease risk and nutritional imbalance; fresh/frozen alternatives strongly preferred. Feed every 2–3 days; avoid overfeeding.

goodWith: robust similarly-sized fish that occupy the bottom and mid-water in a species-specific or biotope setup
larger hillstream loaches (as bottom-dwellers in biotope tanks)
peaceful large catfish (e.g. Mystus, Gagata) in very large tanks — with caution
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed
other Channa species (intense intraspecific and interspecific aggression)
small tetras, rasboras, nano fish, and shrimp

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo for Channa orientalis. Verify URL on FishBase species page before use.

summary: The smallest snakehead (~17 cm), endemic to Sri Lanka, and the only Channa with absent or vestigial pelvic fins. An obligate air-breather requiring unobstructed surface access. IUCN Vulnerable due to narrow island endemism. Import and interstate transport banned in the US under the Lacey Act.
careNotes:
Channa orientalis is the smallest member of the snakehead family and one of the most compact snakeheads in the hobby, reaching a maximum of approximately 17 cm. It is endemic to Sri Lanka and is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List due to its restricted island range and ongoing habitat degradation from agricultural expansion. A key morphological distinction from all other Channa species is the absence or severe reduction of pelvic fins — this is the primary identification character. It should not be confused with Channa ornatipinnis (formerly mislabelled as 'ornaticus' in trade), which is a separate, larger Sri Lankan species.

As an obligate air-breather, the Ceylon Snakehead must have constant access to the water surface to breathe atmospheric air using its suprabranchial (labyrinth-like) organ. A tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable: this species is an adept jumper and will escape through even small gaps. Maintain a humid air gap between the water surface and the lid to prevent respiratory tissue damage from cold or dry air.

Keep singly or as a verified mated pair only. This species is highly territorial and predatory toward any fish small enough to be consumed. A biotope-style setup with soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.5), tannin-stained from driftwood and leaf litter, and heavy floating plant coverage closely replicates Sri Lankan swamp habitat and reduces stress. Avoid bright overhead lighting.

Minimum tank size of approximately 90 L (for a single specimen) is widely cited; a longer footprint (90+ cm) is more important than volume. Weekly partial water changes of 20–30% are recommended to maintain water quality. The species is sensitive to poor water conditions and ammonia spikes.

IMPORTANT — Legal status: All Channa (snakehead) species are classified as Injurious Wildlife by the US Fish & Wildlife Service under the Lacey Act (50 CFR 16.13). Import into the United States and interstate transport are federal offences. Hobbyists outside the US should verify local legislation; several EU member states and other countries maintain similar restrictions on Channidae.
breedingNotes: Channa orientalis exhibits biparental substrate-brooding and mouth-brooding behaviour. The pair construct a loose bubble nest or deposit eggs freely at the water surface among floating vegetation; both parents guard the eggs and subsequently mouth-brood the fry. The male typically takes the primary guarding role. Eggs hatch within 2–4 days at tropical temperatures; fry become free-swimming after roughly a week and accept nauplii (Artemia, microworms) as first foods. Parents generally tolerate fry until they are large enough to be viewed as prey, at which point the juveniles should be removed. Breeding in captivity has been documented but is uncommon; a large tank, a well-matched pair, seasonal temperature fluctuation to simulate dry/wet season cycling, and dense floating-plant coverage improve success.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Channa orientalis
primary.notes: Primary source. Species account for Channa orientalis covering adult size (~17 cm), native range (Sri Lanka endemic), habitat (shallow swamps, rice paddies, irrigation ditches), water parameters (22–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–179 ppm), diet (invertebrates, small fish), tank requirements (90 cm minimum footprint, tight lid), pelvic-fin absence as key ID character, IUCN Vulnerable status, and parental care notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Channa orientalis
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Channidae, Anabantiformes), adult size (max 17 cm TL), native range (Sri Lanka), habitat (demersal, freshwater), diet (carnivore — fish, invertebrates), and temperature tolerance (22–28°C). Confirmed Sri Lanka as sole native country.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Snakehead Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: General snakehead care guide from Aquarium Co-Op. Notes on obligate air-breathing, lid requirements, piscivore diet, tank aggression levels, and Lacey Act legal status in the US. Supplementary trade-side care context.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Channa orientalis profile
additional.notes: Species feature on C. orientalis as the smallest snakehead; highlights pelvic-fin absence as diagnostic character, Sri Lanka endemism, swamp biotope, feeding on invertebrates and small fish, parental care behaviour, and 'Walking Snakehead' common name. Confirms confusion risk with C. ornatipinnis in trade.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Channa orientalis
additional.notes: IUCN Red List assessment: Vulnerable (VU) due to restricted range (Sri Lanka endemic) and ongoing habitat loss from agricultural conversion, drainage, and pollution of wetland habitats.

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental substrate-brooder and mouthbrooder; bubble nest or surface spawn among floating plants, both parents guard and mouth-brood fry. Fry accept Artemia nauplii as first food. Seasonal temperature cycling and large heavily planted tank improve success. Captive breeding documented but uncommon.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: All Channa species are USDA-listed Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act; import and interstate transport banned in the US. Sri Lankan endemic; IUCN Vulnerable due to range restriction.
